title: Product Liability
description: When a defective product causes injury, the manufacturer, distributor, or retailer may be liable under product liability law. California and Arizona recognize three theories of defect: design defects, manufacturing defects, and failure to warn. You do not need to prove negligence — strict liability applies in many product injury cases.
metaTitle: Product Liability Guide — Injured by a Defective Product in CA & AZ
metaDescription: Injured by a defective product in California or Arizona? Learn about strict liability, evidence preservation, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Manufacturing Defects
commonCauses.description: An error in the manufacturing process causes a specific product to deviate from its intended design, creating a dangerous condition.

commonCauses.title: Design Defects
commonCauses.description: The entire product line is inherently dangerous because of a flaw in the design itself — even if manufactured exactly as intended.

commonCauses.title: Failure to Warn
commonCauses.description: A product that poses non-obvious dangers without adequate warnings, instructions, or safety labels can create liability for the manufacturer.

commonCauses.title: Defective Vehicle Components
commonCauses.description: Tire blowouts, brake failures, airbag malfunctions, and other automotive defects cause serious accidents and injuries.

commonCauses.title: Dangerous Consumer Products
commonCauses.description: Household appliances, power tools, children's toys, and electronics that malfunction and cause burns, lacerations, or other injuries.

commonCauses.title: Pharmaceutical and Medical Device Defects
commonCauses.description: Dangerous drug side effects inadequately disclosed, and defective medical devices implanted in patients, are significant sources of product liability claims.

commonCauses.title: Food Contamination
commonCauses.description: Foodborne illness caused by contaminated food products can support product liability claims against manufacturers and distributors.

immediateSteps.title: Seek medical care
immediateSteps.description: Get evaluated and treated for your injuries. Document the cause of injury in your medical records as specifically as possible.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve the defective product
immediateSteps.description: This is the most critical step. Do not discard, repair, or alter the product that caused your injury. It is the primary piece of evidence in a product liability case.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve all packaging and documentation
immediateSteps.description: Keep the original packaging, instructions, warranty cards, and any receipts or purchase records.

immediateSteps.title: Photograph everything
immediateSteps.description: Document the product, the defect (if visible), your injuries, and any damage to property. Photograph from multiple angles.

immediateSteps.title: Check for recalls
immediateSteps.description: Search the product on the CPSC recall database (cpsc.gov) and the manufacturer's website. An existing recall can significantly support your claim.

immediateSteps.title: Identify other potential victims
immediateSteps.description: If you are aware of others injured by the same product, documenting this can support a pattern-of-defect argument or connect you to a broader litigation.

immediateSteps.title: Report to the appropriate agency
immediateSteps.description: Report consumer product injuries to the CPSC (saferproducts.gov). Report pharmaceutical or medical device injuries to the FDA.

immediateSteps.title: Keep all medical and financial records
immediateSteps.description: Document all treatment costs, lost wages, and any other expenses related to the product injury.

items: The defective product itself (do not repair or discard)
Original packaging and instructions
Purchase receipt or proof of purchase
Warranty documentation
Serial number, lot number, and manufacturing date (if visible)
Photos of the product and visible defect
Photos of all injuries
Photos of any property damage caused by the product
Written account of exactly how the product was being used when the injury occurred
CPSC recall search results for the product
Any manufacturer safety notices or bulletins
Evidence of other reported injuries (online reviews, recall databases)
Emergency room or urgent care records
All follow-up physician and specialist notes
Imaging reports
Physical therapy and rehabilitation records
Prescription records and symptom journal
All medical bills
Lost wages documentation
Cost of the product
Any other accident-related out-of-pocket expenses

timelineRisks.risk: Product may be discarded or repaired
timelineRisks.action: Preserve the defective product exactly as it was at the time of injury. This is the most critical step and cannot be undone if missed.
Check for existing recalls. Report your injury to CPSC or FDA as applicable to create an official record.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for product liability personal injury
timelineRisks.action: California has a 2-year personal injury statute of limitations from the date of injury.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for product liability personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Arizona also has a 2-year personal injury statute of limitations for product liability claims.

timelineRisks.risk: Some states have an outer time limit regardless of discovery
timelineRisks.action: Some product liability claims have an outer statute of repose independent of when you discovered the injury. Consult an attorney for your specific situation.

insuranceIssues.explanation: In California and Arizona, manufacturers can be held strictly liable for defective products that cause injury. You do not have to prove the manufacturer was careless — only that the product was defective and caused your injury.

insuranceIssues.issue: Multiple parties in the supply chain
insuranceIssues.explanation: Liability can extend to designers, manufacturers, component part makers, distributors, and retailers. Identifying the right parties is a key step.
Manufacturers may argue the product was used in an unintended way or was altered by the user. Documenting the normal and intended use of the product at the time of injury counters this defense.

insuranceIssues.issue: Class actions and mass tort litigation
insuranceIssues.explanation: If many people have been injured by the same product, class action or mass tort litigation may be pending. An attorney can advise whether joining existing litigation makes sense.

whenToGetLawyer: You were injured by a product that malfunctioned or was defective
The product has an active recall you were not notified about
Your injuries required medical treatment or resulted in significant expenses
A pharmaceutical drug or medical device caused unexpected harm
You want to preserve the evidence before it is compromised
You are aware of others injured by the same product
